name: Master of Architecture and M.S. in Civil Engineering

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: No curriculum is present to evaluate: sampleCourses is empty, only one course code is referenced (referencedCourses 1), and no requirement groups were parsed, so neither the architecture nor the civil-engineering half of this dual degree is documented. Progression cannot be assessed because there is effectively no course content on the page.
dimensions.suggestion: Populate the page with both tracks: the M.Arch studio and technical sequence and the M.S. Civil core (structures, geotechnical, construction management), then show how they share credit.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: There is no usable prerequisite graph: deepestPrereqChain is 0 and the single referenced course (MATH 112) is a dangling, undergraduate-level reference (missingCourseCount 1) rather than a graduate course in the plan. The structure is undefined rather than merely shallow.
dimensions.suggestion: Add the actual graduate course records and prerequisites for both degrees; MATH 112 should appear only as an admission background note, not as the program's lone reference.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No learning outcomes are stated, and with no courses either, the page provides essentially nothing about what the dual degree teaches or produces.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish joint learning outcomes spanning architectural design and civil/structural engineering competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null, summedTermCredits is null, creditReconcileDelta is null, and there are no requirement groups, so there is zero credit transparency for what should be one of the largest dual-degree loads in the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: State the combined credit total and the split between M.Arch and M.S. Civil requirements, highlighting shared credit that shortens the dual path.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The catalog page is effectively a stub: no course descriptions, no plan of study, no credits, and its only concrete reference is a dangling MATH 112. A prospective student learns nothing actionable about the program.
dimensions.suggestion: Build out the page from the two source curricula with labeled tracks, course descriptions, a plan of study, and a credit summary.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The concept, pairing architectural design with civil/structural engineering, is genuinely market-relevant for integrated building design and performance-based practice, but the data contains no courses to demonstrate any current or dated emphasis, so relevance is only presumed from the title.
dimensions.suggestion: Once populated, foreground structural engineering integration, BIM-based structural coordination, and resilient/low-carbon structural design to signal currency.

descriptionQuality.justification: No course descriptions exist in the data (sampleCourses is empty), so description quality cannot be demonstrated and defaults to the lowest score for a catalog page with no course content.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: The only reference is a dangling MATH 112 with no stated grade condition or context, and no graduate-course prerequisites are present, so prerequisite information is essentially absent.

missingTopics.rationale: A combined architecture-civil degree should teach structural analysis, steel/concrete/timber design, and structural detailing so graduates can integrate structure into architectural form; none is present.
Foundations, soil mechanics, and site engineering are core civil competencies that a joint degree must cover but that are entirely absent from the parsed data.
Scheduling, estimating, and construction methods bridge the two disciplines and belong in the plan, yet no such course appears.
Performance under lateral loads is central to structurally literate architecture; a dedicated course is expected and missing.
Digital coordination between architectural and structural models is the primary workflow uniting the two fields and should be an explicit requirement.

emergingTrends.trend: Mass timber and low-carbon structural systems
emergingTrends.rationale: Engineered timber and embodied-carbon-driven structural choices are reshaping practice at exactly the architecture-structure boundary this degree occupies.
Simulation-driven, performance-based engineering is replacing prescriptive design and should anchor the civil-engineering half.
Flood, heat, and seismic resilience are top priorities for buildings and infrastructure, a natural theme for a joint architecture-civil program.

emergingTrends.trend: Digital twins and structural health monitoring
emergingTrends.rationale: Sensor-instrumented digital twins for lifecycle structural monitoring connect design, engineering, and operations.
Generative and AI-based structural optimization tools are entering practice and should be introduced critically in a modern joint curriculum.

topSuggestions: Populate the currently empty catalog page with both the M.Arch and M.S. Civil Engineering curricula, including course descriptions and a plan of study.
State the combined total credits and the credit split/overlap between the two degrees.
Replace the lone dangling MATH 112 reference with the actual graduate course requirements and prerequisites.
Publish joint learning outcomes covering both architectural design and structural/civil engineering competencies.
Once built out, foreground structural integration, resilience, and BIM-based structural coordination to establish currency.
